item.title: The __set_name__ method for descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
DeôÙscripôÙtors genôÙerôÙalôÙly have to inôÙterôÙact with atôÙtributes of the manôÙaged obôÙjecôÙt, and this is done by inôÙspectôÙing __ôÙdicôÙt__ on that obôÙject (or callôÙing getatôÙtr/seôÙtatôÙtr, but the probôÙlem is the same), and findôÙing the key unôÙderôÙ the speôÙcifôÙic name.

For this reaôÙson, the deôÙscripôÙtor will have to know the name of the key to look ôÙfor, which is reôÙlatôÙed to the name of the atôÙtribute is manôÙagôÙing.

On preôÙviôÙous verôÙsions of Python this had to be done exôÙplicôÙitôÙly. If we wantôÙed to ôÙwork around it, there were some more adôÙvanced ways to do so. LuckôÙiôÙly, afôÙter PEP-487 (added in Python 3.6), there are some enôÙhanceôÙments reôÙgardôÙing class creôÙation, which alôÙso afôÙfectôÙs deôÙscripôÙtors.

Let's reôÙview the probôÙlem, the preôÙviôÙous apôÙproachôÙes to tackôÙle it, and the modôÙern way of solvôÙing it.

Read moreãÎ (4 min remaining to read)

item.title: Descriptors & Decorators
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
DeôÙscripôÙtors are an amazôÙing tool to have in our toolôÙbox, as they come in handy in many opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙties.

ProbôÙaôÙbly the best thing about deôÙscripôÙtors, is that they can imôÙprove othôÙer ôÙsoôÙluôÙtionôÙs. Let's see how we can write betôÙter decôÙoôÙraôÙtors, by usôÙing deôÙscripôÙtors.

Read moreãÎ (5 min remaining to read)

item.title: Types of Descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
ReôÙsumôÙing from where we left ofôÙf, on the preôÙviôÙous post, on which we took A first look at deôÙscripôÙtors, it's time to exôÙplore their difôÙferôÙent typeôÙs and how they work inôÙterôÙnalôÙly.

In Python, almost everything is represented with a dictionary. Objects are dictionaries. Classes are objects, hence they also are contained into a dictionary. This is denoted by the __dict__ attribute that objects have.

There are two types of descriptors: data descriptors and non-data ones. If a descriptor implements both [1] __get__() and __set__(), it's called a data descriptor; otherwise is a non-data descriptor.

Read moreãÎ (7 min remaining to read)

item.title: A first look at descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
DeôÙscripôÙtors are one of the most powôÙerôÙful feaôÙtures of Python. The reaôÙson why they're so powôÙerôÙful is beôÙcause they enôÙable us to conôÙtrol the core opôÙerôÙaôÙtions (get, set, delete) [1], of an atôÙtribute in a givôÙen obôÙjecôÙt, so that we can hook a parôÙticôÙuôÙlar code, conôÙtrolled by us, in orôÙder to modôÙiôÙfy, change, or exôÙtend the oôÙrigôÙiôÙnal opôÙerôÙaôÙtion.

Read moreãÎ (8 min remaining to read)

item.title: Upcoming talk at PyCon CZ 2017
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
The proôÙposôÙal of my talk tiôÙtled "DisôÙcovôÙerôÙing DeôÙscripôÙtors", was acôÙceptôÙed for the next ediôÙtion of PyôÙCon CZ, in Prague, Czech ReôÙpubôÙlic. AlôÙthough the schedôÙule is not yet ready, this talk will most likeôÙly be on June 8th.

The first two days of the conôÙferôÙence (June 8th and June 9th), will be for talkôÙs, whilst the last one (SatôÙurôÙday, June 10), will be for workôÙshops (sprints).

This is anôÙothôÙer inôÙterôÙestôÙing opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to share and learn from the Python ôÙcomôÙmuôÙniôÙty, as it was last year when I preôÙsentôÙed at EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016. On this ocôÙcaôÙsion, I ôÙplan to post an enôÙtry with the events, per each day of the conôÙferôÙence.

item.title: __wrapped__ in Python decorators
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This is anôÙothôÙer of the new inôÙterôÙestôÙing things that Python 3 has. Time ago, it was someôÙhow tricky to work with decôÙoôÙratôÙed funcôÙtionôÙs, beôÙcause the decôÙoôÙraôÙtor reôÙplaced the origôÙiôÙnal obôÙjecôÙt, and its beôÙhavôÙiour beôÙcame hard to reach.

Read moreãÎ (2 min remaining to read)

item.title: Python Barcelona November meetup notes
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Last ThursôÙday NoôÙvemôÙber 17, 2016, there was a Python meetôÙup in Barcelona [1], with a set of inôÙterôÙestôÙing talkôÙs.

The first one was hostôÙed by two repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtives of the govôÙernôÙment of CatalunôÙya, and Barcelona city, and they preôÙsentôÙed the techôÙniôÙcal chalôÙlenges they are ôÙfacôÙing, and the new stack proôÙposed for onôÙcomôÙing projectôÙs. In this reôÙgard, it was inôÙterôÙestôÙing to see how they have lots of legaôÙcy apôÙpliôÙcaôÙtions writôÙten inôÙ J2EE, with JaôÙva frameôÙworkôÙs, that are outôÙdatôÙed, difôÙfiôÙcult to mainôÙtain, and they ôÙmenôÙtioned the idea of miôÙgratôÙing them to new, more modôÙern techôÙnoloôÙgies. In this sense, there are alôÙready projects in proôÙgress, and they chose Python + DjanôÙgo ôÙfor the miôÙgraôÙtion and re-imôÙpleôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion of the legaôÙcy sysôÙtemôÙs.

This was a very inôÙterôÙestôÙing in the sense that more than this was acôÙtuôÙalôÙly pôÙreôÙsentôÙed. In parôÙticôÙuôÙlar the idea of how the govôÙernôÙment wants to acôÙtuôÙalôÙly own their sysôÙtemôÙs, and thereôÙfore they are now choosôÙing open source softôÙware. It ôÙgoes beôÙyond than mereôÙly usôÙing Python and DjanôÙgo: they are alôÙso miôÙgratôÙing the ôÙworkôÙstaôÙtion maôÙchines to open source softôÙware (UbunôÙtu), and tryôÙing to earn more ôÙconôÙtracôÙtors that are starôÙtups rather than huge multiôÙnaôÙtionôÙals with proôÙpriôÙetary ôÙsoftôÙware. The idea is still the same: they want open data, transôÙparenôÙcy, and to acôÙtuôÙalôÙly own their sysôÙtemôÙs.

These sort of iniôÙtiaôÙtives are gainôÙing more and more tracôÙtion in the EuôÙroôÙpean UôÙnion, as many cities are startôÙing to shift toôÙwards an open govôÙernôÙmenôÙt, withôÙ open data, and more transôÙparenôÙcy. I look forôÙward to seeôÙing more projects inôÙ Python with open source techôÙnoloôÙgies, at their GitHub acôÙcount [2], in the ôÙforthôÙcomôÙing monthôÙs.

The secôÙond talk was about ConôÙda, and shortôÙly thereôÙafter a brief inôÙtroôÙducôÙtion, the rest of the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion was ôÙmostôÙly a deôÙmo (live codôÙing! :-), on which we saw sevôÙerôÙal exôÙamôÙples of inôÙstalling ôÙpackôÙages, exôÙportôÙing the enôÙviôÙronôÙmenôÙt, creôÙatôÙing a new one from a temôÙplate, etc. It was useôÙful to see both the strengths and shortôÙcomôÙings of the tool, what can and ôÙcanôÙnot be done with it, and how it can be useôÙful for deôÙvelôÙopôÙers that have to deal with sevôÙerôÙal sysôÙtem deôÙpenôÙdenôÙcies.

The third and last talk, was about PLONE, a CMS proôÙjecôÙt writôÙten in Python, and with its 15-year-old tenure, is the oldôÙest Python proôÙject (thing that I did not know). It was inôÙterôÙestôÙing to learn about itôÙs highôÙ-levôÙel arôÙchiôÙtecôÙture, comôÙpoôÙnents, and the way they work. Above that, I would ôÙlike to highôÙlight the reôÙcap that it was done about many unôÙderôÙapôÙpreôÙciôÙatôÙed zope liôÙbraries, that have been availôÙable in Python for a long time, doôÙing an amazôÙing job. It is now in my list, to reôÙview them.

All in alôÙl, it was a good meeôÙtup, and I liked very much the techôÙniôÙcal levôÙel of the talkôÙs, and the fact that the topôÙics were very diôÙverse, yet inôÙterôÙestôÙing. I would like to conôÙtinôÙue atôÙtendôÙing these meeôÙtupôÙs, and in the ôÙfuôÙture I might even subôÙmit a talk.

[1]https://www.meetup.com/es-ES/python-185/events/235058828/
[2]https://github.com/AjuntamentdeBarcelona

item.title: Oncoming events of Python and Architecture in September
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
The next SatôÙurôÙday 10 of SeptemôÙber, I will be preôÙsentôÙing my talk "Clean Code in Python", as preôÙsentôÙed in the preôÙviôÙous EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016, at a loôÙcal Python event in Cû°rôÙdoba, ArôÙgentiôÙna [1] [2].

The folôÙlowôÙing week, I will be atôÙtendôÙing a loôÙcal arôÙchiôÙtecôÙture meetôÙup (arôÙqôÙconôÙf), on which a talk [3] about OpenôÙStack will be hostôÙed, exôÙplainôÙing how they work in a disôÙtribôÙuted fashôÙion.

I look forôÙward to enôÙjoyôÙing both events!

[1]https://www.meetup.com/Cordoba-Python-Meetup/events/233741709/
[2]https://medium.com/@toniabdala/juntada-python-c%C3%B3rdoba-septiembre-db4abe5c4858#.yo0x1mqe3
[3]https://www.meetup.com/ArquitecturaDeSoftware/events/233824352/

item.title: My talk @ EuroPython 2016
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
I had the great exôÙpeôÙriôÙence of preôÙsentôÙing at EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016. My talk enôÙtiôÙtled "Clean code in Python", was about good deôÙvelôÙopôÙment pracôÙtices, down to low-levôÙel deôÙsign (with code exôÙamôÙples), for Python. The idea of the talk, was to present the "pythonôÙic" apôÙproach for writôÙing code, and how do genôÙerôÙal conôÙcepts of ôÙclean code apôÙply to Python.

These exôÙamôÙples might be useôÙful for beôÙginôÙnerôÙs, deôÙvelôÙopôÙers exôÙpeôÙriôÙenced in othôÙer lanôÙguages comôÙing to Python, and peoôÙple usôÙing Python for sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic apôÙpliôÙcaôÙtionôÙs. The exôÙamôÙples could alôÙso be helpôÙful for seôÙnior deôÙvelôÙopôÙerôÙs, beôÙcause they reôÙmind reôÙal sitôÙuôÙaôÙtions that might apôÙpear in pull reôÙquestôÙs, while doôÙing a code reôÙview.

Here is the video on YouTube:

And the slides (which I alôÙso made availôÙable along with the source code, shortôÙly afôÙter the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion finôÙished).

The preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion was well reôÙceived: some atôÙtenôÙdees ôÙtold me they liked it (even asked for the slides and code), and I got good adôÙvices. The folôÙlowôÙing days of the conôÙferôÙence, more peoôÙple told me that they liked the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion, and some othôÙerôÙs ôÙmenôÙtioned (someôÙthing I did not think at the beôÙginôÙning, but that it makes perôÙfect sense), that these ideas are reôÙalôÙly useôÙful for peoôÙple usôÙing Python in sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic enôÙviôÙronôÙments.

I am glad it was useôÙful for the comôÙmuôÙniôÙty.

item.title: EuroPython 2016 remarks
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Last week, EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016 finôÙished, and it was an amazôÙing conôÙferôÙence I had the pleaôÙsure to atôÙtend. Here is my reôÙview of those days.

The conference

I arôÙrived on SatôÙurôÙday noon at BilôÙbao, Spain, the day beôÙfore the conôÙferôÙence, so I had some time to know the city, see the venues, etc. The next day, on SunôÙday, was for two sepôÙaôÙrate workôÙshopôÙs: DjanôÙgo girls and BeôÙginôÙner's day. I atôÙtendôÙed the beôÙginôÙner's day as a coach, and helped a group of inôÙterôÙmeôÙdiôÙate deôÙvelôÙopôÙers withôÙ ôÙsevôÙerôÙal exôÙerôÙcisôÙes aimed at exôÙplainôÙing some Python conôÙceptôÙs, such as: conôÙtext manôÙagerôÙs, decôÙoôÙraôÙtors, ôÙmagôÙic methôÙodôÙs, genôÙerôÙaôÙtors, etc. It was reôÙalôÙly cuôÙriôÙous that some of these topôÙics were those I was goôÙing to covôÙer on my talk on WednesôÙday, so I felt reôÙalôÙly glad about that. I took an oath (very funôÙny BTW) for beôÙcomôÙing a beôÙginôÙner's menôÙtor, and so I did (it was reôÙalôÙly good acôÙtuôÙalôÙly). I had a great time helpôÙing othôÙer deôÙvelôÙopôÙerôÙs, exôÙchangôÙing ideas and exôÙpeôÙriôÙences durôÙing lunch, ôÙsolvôÙing probôÙlemôÙs, and getôÙting a first glimpse on what the conôÙferôÙence was goôÙing to be like.

The moôÙment of the oath for beôÙcomôÙing a menôÙtor, and earnôÙing the badge.

AfôÙter the workôÙshop finôÙished, I walked to the main venue, and gave a hand packôÙing the bags of the conôÙferôÙence. AfôÙter that, time to see around BilôÙbao.

From MonôÙday to FriôÙday was the conôÙferôÙence itôÙself, with all the talkôÙs, and trainôÙings.

MonôÙday startôÙed with the inôÙtroôÙducôÙtion to the conôÙferôÙence, and shortôÙly thereôÙafter, the very first keyôÙnote by Rachel Willmer, who gave a great preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion, sharôÙing a lot of exôÙpeôÙriôÙence, and inôÙterôÙestôÙing ideas.

At around noon there was a keyôÙnote by N. TollerôÙvey about MiôÙcroPyôÙthon. The preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion was exôÙcelôÙlent (one of the ones I liked the most), and the idea of the project is aweôÙsome. On top of that, it was anôÙnounced that the BBC was ôÙgivôÙing away miôÙcro:bits for the atôÙtenôÙdees of the conôÙferôÙence, so it was a great surôÙprise to pick up mine at the conôÙferôÙence desk. I even startôÙed playôÙing around a bit with it (more in a fuôÙture post).

The rest of the afôÙterôÙnoon, I atôÙtendôÙed sevôÙerôÙal talkôÙs. At the end, there were, of course the lightôÙning talkôÙs, which were amazôÙing.

TuesôÙday startôÙed with the keyôÙnote by P. HildeôÙbranôÙt, preôÙsentôÙing how DisôÙney usôÙes sevôÙerôÙal techôÙnoloôÙgies, inôÙcludôÙing Python, as supôÙport for movies and proôÙducôÙtionôÙs. It was very good and enôÙlightôÙenôÙing to see an enôÙdeavôÙour of such exôÙtent with Python. After that, durôÙing mornôÙing I atôÙtendôÙed a workôÙshop about Async web deôÙvelôÙopôÙmenôÙt, with sevôÙerôÙal Python techôÙnoloôÙgies ôÙfor doôÙing asynôÙchroôÙnous comôÙpuôÙtaôÙtion.

DurôÙing the afôÙterôÙnoon, I watched sevôÙerôÙal great talkôÙs, inôÙcludôÙing "ProôÙtect you users with CirôÙcuit BreakôÙerôÙs", and ôÙsevôÙerôÙal othôÙer good ones, closôÙing with the lightôÙning talkôÙs.

WednesôÙday was the day of my talk, so I atôÙtendôÙed some talks durôÙing mornôÙing and then, at the afôÙterôÙnoon, I preôÙsentôÙed mine. I reôÙalôÙly liked how it deôÙvelôÙopeôÙd. MoreôÙover, it was reôÙalôÙly good to reôÙceive good feedôÙback from some atôÙtenôÙdees, sayôÙing they ôÙliked it, and that it was useôÙful for them. ShortôÙly thereôÙafter, I pubôÙlished the slides and the source code.

On ThursôÙday, there were some talks about async/await and asynôÙchroôÙnous proôÙgramôÙming in Python 3, mockôÙs, and highôÙ-availôÙabilôÙiôÙty arôÙchiôÙtecôÙture.

On FriôÙday, the keyôÙnote was about how Python is used by the sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic comôÙmuôÙniôÙty. It was very enôÙlightôÙenôÙing, and inôÙterôÙestôÙing ôÙto see anôÙothôÙer use case of Python, and how is beôÙcomôÙing the main techôÙnolôÙoôÙgy on this area.

The talks durôÙing mornôÙing in this case, were diôÙvidôÙed among sevôÙerôÙal topôÙicôÙs, beôÙing the main ones: inôÙstruôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion for perôÙforôÙmance ôÙmetôÙricôÙs, "How to miôÙgrate form PostôÙgreSQL to HDôÙF5 and live hapôÙpiôÙly evôÙer afôÙter", "SôÙplit Up! FightôÙing the monoôÙlith". DurôÙing the afôÙterôÙnoon, I joined a workôÙshop about DockôÙer, on which we built an apôÙpliôÙcaôÙtion usôÙing DockôÙer-ôÙcomôÙbine, and folôÙlowed good pracôÙtices.

It is worth menôÙtionôÙing, that on FriôÙday there was an speôÙcial ediôÙtion for lightôÙning talkôÙs, which was not in the origôÙiôÙnal schedôÙule. AfôÙter ôÙmakôÙing some arôÙrangeôÙments, and due to some on-the-fly changes, it was posôÙsiôÙble to have anôÙothôÙer sesôÙsion for lightôÙning talkôÙs, right beôÙfore the sprints oriôÙenôÙtaôÙtion and the closôÙing sesôÙsion.

SatôÙurôÙday and SunôÙday were for sprints (hackathonôÙs). On SatôÙurôÙday I joined to sprint on aioôÙhttp, and acôÙtuôÙalôÙly ôÙsubôÙmitôÙted a pull reôÙquest, that was merged, whereôÙas on SunôÙday I wantôÙed to check on a pytest isôÙsue.

Sprints @euôÙropyôÙthon! pic.twitôÙter.ôÙcom/PVQZw9rSvV

ã PySS SoôÙciôÙety (@acpyss) July 23, 2016

My talk

It was great to have the opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to present at EuôÙroPyôÙthon. What was even betôÙter, was the posôÙiôÙtive feedôÙback I got from othôÙer atôÙtenôÙdees, and the fact that it was useôÙful and inôÙterôÙestôÙing for them (which was, in the end, what I cared most about). I found the exôÙpeôÙriôÙence very ôÙposôÙiôÙtive.

From the comôÙments, I gathôÙered someôÙthing I have not noôÙticed when I first enôÙviôÙsioned the talk, which is how useôÙful these conôÙcepts might be for peoôÙple usôÙing Python for sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic apôÙpliôÙcaôÙtionôÙs. It seemôÙs, sciôÙenôÙtists usôÙing Python for daôÙta proôÙcessôÙing or comôÙpuôÙtaôÙtion, do not usuôÙalôÙly have the backôÙground of a deôÙvelôÙopôÙer, so conôÙcepts like code readôÙabilôÙiôÙty, techôÙniôÙcal debt, and mainôÙtainôÙabilôÙiôÙty, are helpôÙful in orôÙder ôÙto imôÙprove the code base. This gave me the idea of adaptôÙing the exôÙamôÙples, perôÙhaps adding one reôÙlatôÙed to these arôÙeas.

Python use cases

There were peoôÙple from many counôÙtries, inôÙdusôÙtries, and comôÙpaôÙnies with difôÙferôÙent backôÙgroundôÙs. The trend seems to be now on ôÙdaôÙta sciôÙence, but Python is wideôÙly used in many arôÙeas.

I beôÙlieve the main arôÙeas of foôÙcus for Python are: softôÙware deôÙvelôÙopôÙmenôÙt, sysôÙtem adôÙminôÙisôÙtraôÙtion / Dev OpôÙs, and sciôÙence.

There were talkôÙs, trackôÙs, sesôÙsionôÙs, and trainôÙings for all of them, with very techôÙniôÙcal deôÙtail.

Highlights

There were so many great talks and reôÙsources that I canôÙnot name each sinôÙgle one of them, so I will point the main ôÙtopics and some of the talks that grabbed my atôÙtenôÙtion the most, but please keep in mind that all were great.

Among the many things pendôÙing to test and reôÙsearch, are alôÙso bookôÙs. I learned about PYôÙRO4, for manôÙagôÙing Python reôÙmote obôÙjectôÙs, which seems like a promisôÙing techôÙnolôÙoôÙgy. I will dive inôÙto more deôÙtail on conôÙda and the buildôÙing sysôÙtemôÙs, conôÙda chanôÙnelôÙs, etc. The talk "ExôÙplorôÙing your Python inôÙterôÙpreter" was reôÙalôÙly inôÙterôÙestôÙing, and it was a good inôÙtroôÙducôÙtion, in orôÙder ôÙto beôÙcome inôÙvolved with CPython deôÙvelôÙopôÙmenôÙt.

I atôÙtendôÙed many talks about the latôÙest feaôÙtures of Python 3.5, such as asynôÙcIO, corouôÙtiôÙnes, and all the new funcôÙtionôÙalôÙiôÙties for asynôÙchronous proôÙgramôÙming, and they all were reôÙalôÙly inôÙterôÙestôÙing. In parôÙticôÙuôÙlar "The reôÙport of TwistôÙed's Death" was very inôÙterôÙestôÙing, and (spoilôÙer alert), it looks like still has an inôÙterôÙestôÙing fuôÙture comôÙpetôÙing with the new liôÙbraries and stanôÙdardôÙs.

On the lightôÙning talkôÙs, it was preôÙsentôÙed a reôÙverse deôÙbugôÙger (revdôÙb), and its deôÙmo was amazôÙing.

Conclusion

AfôÙter atôÙtendôÙing many talkôÙs, and trainôÙings, talkôÙing to many othôÙer exôÙpeôÙriôÙence deôÙvelôÙopôÙerôÙs, sysôÙtem adôÙminôÙisôÙtraôÙtors, and daôÙta sciôÙenôÙtistôÙs, I can state that the conôÙferôÙence has an amazôÙing learnôÙing enôÙviôÙronôÙmenôÙt, and the outôÙcome was comôÙpleteôÙly posôÙiôÙtive. It was useôÙful ôÙfor catchôÙing up with techôÙnolôÙoôÙgy, checkôÙing the enôÙviôÙronôÙment and see how Python is beôÙing used or deôÙployed in the wild, learn from use casôÙes, exôÙpeôÙriôÙences, and exôÙchange ideas.

The conôÙtent was reôÙalôÙly inôÙspirôÙing and open-ôÙmindôÙing. I have lots of items to check, as points for reôÙsearch, which I will covôÙer in folôÙlowôÙing enôÙtries.

Python 3 is much more wideôÙly used than one would exôÙpecôÙt. It is acôÙtuôÙalôÙly the stanôÙdard now, and many talks (inôÙcludôÙing mine), were usôÙing Python 3 ôÙcode, but most imôÙporôÙtantôÙly, most projects are now in this verôÙsion, whereôÙas Python 2 looks like the legaôÙcy opôÙtion. Good news :-)

All in alôÙl, this ediôÙtion of EuôÙroPyôÙthon was aweôÙsome, and I am lookôÙing forôÙward to preôÙsentôÙing again next year!
